lessons.title: Chat Agent Design: Persona, Scope, and Escalation Rules
lessons.title_es: Diseño de Agente de Chat: Persona, Alcance y Reglas de Escalación
lessons.description: Define a chat agent persona that converts without sounding robotic, set hard scope boundaries, and build the escalation logic that hands off to humans gracefully.
lessons.description_es: Define una persona de agente de chat que convierte sin sonar robótico, establece límites de alcance estrictos y construye la lógica de escalación que transfiere a humanos con gracia.
lessons.content:
The Persona Framework

A chat agent's persona is not a name and an avatar. It is a defined set of communication rules that determine how the agent sounds in every situation. Agents without a defined persona default to generic, corporate, lifeless responses — which kill conversion. Build your persona using the STAR framework:

  • Style: Tone and voice. Example: "Warm but professional. Direct. Short sentences. Never uses jargon without explaining it. Never says 'Great question!' or 'Certainly!'"
  • Traits: Personality attributes. Example: "Patient, curious about the customer's actual problem before offering solutions, confident about what we can do, honest about what we can't."
  • Aim: The agent's primary goal in every conversation. Example: "Understand the visitor's need and connect them with the right next step — whether that's a demo, a resource, or a human conversation."
  • Rules: Hard constraints. Example: "Never make up pricing. Never promise timelines. Never denigrate competitors. If asked about a topic outside your knowledge, say so and offer to connect with a human."

Scope Definition: What the Agent Can and Cannot Handle

Scope definition is the most under-invested part of chat agent design. An agent without clear scope will hallucinate answers to questions it shouldn't be answering. Define scope in two lists:

In-scope topics: Product features and pricing tiers, common objections and FAQs, booking demos, answering "which plan is right for me" questions, qualifying leads with 3–5 questions, routing to correct department.

Out-of-scope topics: Custom pricing negotiations, technical implementation questions beyond basic FAQ, legal and compliance questions, complaints from existing customers (route to support), anything requiring access to account data.

Escalation Logic: The Three Triggers

Every chat agent needs three escalation triggers that hand the conversation to a human immediately:

  • Explicit request: Visitor says "talk to a human", "speak to someone", "real person". Route instantly, no retention attempt.
  • Frustration signals: Short, terse responses, repeated questions the agent failed to answer, words like "this is useless", "you're not helping". Route with a handoff message: "I want to make sure you get the right answer — let me connect you with someone from our team."
  • High-value signals: Visitor mentions large team size, specific enterprise requirements, or says they're evaluating multiple vendors. Route to a senior rep with a summary of the conversation so far.

The handoff message matters. Don't say "transferring to an agent" — say "I'm going to connect you with [Rep Name] — they'll have our full conversation so you won't need to repeat anything."

lessons.title: Training Your Agent: Knowledge Base, FAQs, and Retrieval-Augmented Generation
lessons.title_es: Entrenando tu Agente: Base de Conocimiento, FAQs y Generación Aumentada por Recuperación
lessons.description: Build the knowledge base that powers accurate agent responses, structure it for RAG retrieval, and measure answer accuracy before going live.
lessons.description_es: Construye la base de conocimiento que impulsa respuestas precisas del agente, estructúrala para recuperación RAG y mide la precisión de las respuestas antes de salir en vivo.
lessons.content:
What RAG Is and Why It Matters

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the architecture that allows a chat agent to give accurate, specific answers about your business without hallucinating. Without RAG, the agent knows only what's in its training data — which doesn't include your pricing, your case studies, or your specific product features. With RAG, the agent searches your knowledge base in real time and uses the retrieved content to ground its response.

How it works: visitor asks a question → question is converted to a vector embedding → embedding is compared against your knowledge base embeddings → top 3–5 most similar chunks are retrieved → those chunks are injected into the LLM prompt as context → LLM generates an answer grounded in your specific content.

Building a RAG-Ready Knowledge Base

RAG quality depends entirely on knowledge base quality. Structure each document for retrieval:

  • One topic per document: Don't put your entire pricing page into one document. Split it: one document per plan, one for the comparison table, one for the FAQ. Smaller, focused chunks retrieve more accurately.
  • Include question-answer pairs: Write explicit Q&A pairs for the 30 most common chat questions. These match visitor question embeddings precisely and retrieve with high confidence.
  • Use consistent terminology: If your product is called "NetWeb CRM" everywhere, don't also call it "the platform" or "the tool" in some documents. Inconsistent terminology fragments your embeddings.
  • Date your content: Add a metadata field for last_updated. Stale pricing or deprecated features erode trust fast. Review and update quarterly.

Content Categories to Include

  • Pricing pages (full text, not just numbers — include what's included at each tier)
  • Feature descriptions with use cases
  • Common objections and rebuttals
  • Customer case studies (3-sentence format: industry, challenge, result)
  • Integration lists with compatible tools
  • Onboarding process description
  • Support SLA and policy documents

Measuring Answer Accuracy Before Launch

Create a golden test set: 50 question-answer pairs where you know the correct answer. Run them through your agent before launch and measure: answer accuracy (did the agent give the right information?), citation rate (did the agent cite a specific document?), and refusal rate (did the agent correctly say "I don't know" for out-of-scope questions?). Target: >90% accuracy, >80% citation rate, 100% correct refusals. Fix low-accuracy topics by improving the relevant knowledge base documents, not by tweaking the LLM prompt.

lessons.title: Deploying Across Channels: Web, WhatsApp, and SMS
lessons.title_es: Despliegue en Múltiples Canales: Web, WhatsApp y SMS
lessons.description: Deploy the same agent brain across web chat, WhatsApp Business, and SMS with channel-specific adaptations for format and user expectations.
lessons.description_es: Despliega el mismo cerebro del agente en chat web, WhatsApp Business y SMS con adaptaciones específicas por canal para formato y expectativas del usuario.
lessons.content:
One Brain, Multiple Channels

The architecture goal is a single agent brain (one system prompt, one knowledge base, one escalation logic) deployed across multiple channels with channel-specific formatting adapters. This prevents drift — if you maintain separate agents per channel, they give inconsistent answers over time.

The channel adapter sits between your agent core and the channel's API. It handles: message formatting (WhatsApp supports bold with asterisks; SMS supports plain text only), session management (web chat can store conversation context in a browser session; SMS uses the phone number as the session key), and attachment handling (WhatsApp can send PDFs and images; SMS cannot).

Web Chat Configuration

Web chat is the highest-context channel — you know the visitor's current page, their scroll behavior, and their session history. Use this data to pre-populate agent context:

  • If visitor is on the pricing page: agent opens with "Comparing our plans? I can help you find the right fit." (not the generic greeting)
  • If visitor has visited 3+ times without converting: agent proactively offers a demo booking
  • If visitor is a returning customer (recognized by email cookie): agent uses their name and references their account

Install the chat widget by adding one script tag. Configure trigger rules under Channels → Web Chat → Trigger Rules: appear after 30 seconds, or when user scrolls 60% of the page, or when exit intent is detected (mouse moves toward browser close button).

WhatsApp Business API Setup

WhatsApp requires a Meta Business Account and WhatsApp Business API access. The setup sequence:

  • Register a dedicated phone number (not your personal number) for the business WhatsApp account
  • Verify your business in Meta Business Manager
  • Apply for WhatsApp Business API access (approval takes 1–5 business days)
  • Connect the approved number to your agent platform via the WhatsApp webhook URL
  • Create message templates for the first outbound message (WhatsApp requires pre-approved templates for business-initiated conversations; conversation-initiated responses have no template requirement)

SMS Integration

SMS via Twilio: provision a local number (not toll-free — higher deliverability), set the inbound webhook to your agent endpoint, and configure opt-in capture. Critical compliance: you must capture explicit SMS opt-in before sending any messages — a web form checkbox with "I agree to receive SMS messages" language is sufficient. Store opt-in timestamp and source URL. Never send marketing SMS to numbers that opted in only for transactional messages. Keep messages under 160 characters to avoid splitting into multiple SMS segments, which increases cost and degrades readability.

lessons.title: Measuring Conversion: Agent Analytics and Optimization Loops
lessons.title_es: Midiendo la Conversión: Analítica del Agente y Ciclos de Optimización
lessons.description: Track the five metrics that determine agent ROI and run monthly optimization loops that compound improvement over time.
lessons.description_es: Rastrea las cinco métricas que determinan el ROI del agente y ejecuta ciclos de optimización mensuales que acumulan mejoras con el tiempo.
lessons.content:
The Five Agent ROI Metrics

Most teams track "chats handled" and "average response time" — which are activity metrics, not ROI metrics. The five metrics that connect your agent to revenue:

  • Conversion Rate (CVR): Of visitors who engage with the agent, what percentage take the desired action (book demo, sign up, request quote)? Benchmark: 8–15% for a well-configured agent. Below 5% = the agent is not understanding or addressing purchase intent.
  • Containment Rate: Of total chats, what percentage were resolved by the AI without escalating to a human? Benchmark: 70–80%. Below 60% = knowledge base gaps or scope too narrow. Above 90% = possibly missing escalation triggers (check customer satisfaction scores).
  • Human Handoff Satisfaction (CSAT post-handoff): Survey customers after human handoff. A low score here (below 7/10) means the handoff is rough — agent isn't passing sufficient context to the human rep.
  • Lead Quality Score: Of leads captured by the agent, what's their average lead score in the CRM? Compare against manually captured leads. If agent-captured leads score lower, your qualification questions are too weak.
  • Revenue Attributed: Of deals closed in a period, what percentage had a chat interaction in the first 14 days? Track this as "agent-influenced revenue" in your CRM. This is the number that justifies the investment.

The Monthly Optimization Loop

Run this four-step process every month:

  • Step 1 — Review failed conversations: Filter chats where the visitor disengaged without converting or explicitly expressed frustration. Read them manually (aim for 20–30). Categorize failure modes: unanswered question, wrong answer, confusing response, missed escalation trigger.
  • Step 2 — Update the knowledge base: For every unanswered question you found, add a Q&A pair to the knowledge base. For every wrong answer, correct the source document.
  • Step 3 — Refine escalation triggers: Add any new frustration language patterns you observed to the escalation trigger list. Remove triggers that are firing too aggressively (visitors hitting handoff when they're actually satisfied).
  • Step 4 — A/B test one element: Change one variable per month — opening message, qualification question order, escalation timing, or CTA button copy. Measure for 2 weeks before declaring a winner. Never change multiple elements simultaneously.

The agents that drive the most revenue are not the most sophisticated — they're the ones that have been iterated on consistently for 6+ months. Build the process, not just the agent.
